app.name: SALIN-LAHI
app.subtitle: Olongapo City Digital Heritage Platform
app.program: DOST Smart City Program · Modern Library Movement

city.name: Olongapo City
city.description: Olongapo City is an independent component city located in the province of Zambales in Central Luzon, Philippines. Strategically situated along the eastern shore of Subic Bay, it is one of the most progressive cities in the Philippines — shaped by its deep indigenous Aeta roots, Spanish colonial history, American naval presence, and modern transformation into a world-class freeport and smart city.

sections.title: Aeta History
sections.photo_caption: The Aeta — first people of the Philippine archipelago
sections.content: Arriving in Luzon roughly 50,000 years ago via ancient land bridges exposed during the Pleistocene Ice Age, the ancestors of the Aeta became the first humans to settle the Philippine archipelago. Following the arrival of agricultural Austronesian migrants around 6,000 years ago, they intentionally retreated into the rugged highlands of Luzon to preserve their unique mobile lifestyle and genetic homogeneity. This deep-rooted resilience faced a modern crisis in June 1991 when the violent eruption of Mount Pinatubo permanently displaced many Aeta communities from their traditional mountain sanctuaries into new resettlement zones.

fun_facts.title: Ice Age Trailblazers
fun_facts.text: The Aeta arrived in the Philippines during the last Ice Age when massive glaciers locked up the oceans, physically lowering sea levels to expose the walkable land pathways they traveled.

fun_facts.title: The Highland Retreat
fun_facts.text: Instead of clashing destructively with incoming Austronesian farmers, the Aeta chose to move deep into the Zambales, Bataan, and Sierra Madre mountain ranges to deliberately shield their culture and ancestry from outside mixing.

sections.title: Traditional Livelihood
sections.photo_caption: Traditional Aeta livelihood and community life
sections.content: The traditional music of the Aeta serves as a vital spiritual utility and an oral historical archive, utilizing instruments like bamboo tube zithers (kabungbung), nose flutes (bansik), and flat gongs (gassa) forged entirely from jungle materials. These sonic elements come together during the Anituan healing séance, a dramatic group ritual where a spiritual medium enters a trance to communicate with ancestral spirits (anitos). Through rhythmic dancing and hypnotic instrumental drones, the community creates a sensory environment designed to identify and cast away negative frequencies causing physical or psychological illness.

fun_facts.title: The Bird-Mimicking Flute
fun_facts.text: The bansik is a highly specialized bamboo nose flute featuring precision-cut tonal holes designed explicitly to mirror the complex calls of forest birds.

fun_facts.title: Open-Palm Rhythms
fun_facts.text: Unlike traditional Western or Asian gongs that require mallets, the sacred gassa gong is played entirely with the bare open palm to create steady, earth-grounding cadences.

questions.q: What is a kabungbung?
What is the Anituan?
What type of flute is the bansik?
What do the ancestral spirits communicate through during the Anituan?
What are the ancestral spirits called in Aeta tradition?

sections.title: Traditional Tools
sections.photo_caption: Aeta traditional hunting tools and bow-and-arrow craft
sections.content: The Aeta engineered a highly advanced bow and arrow complex individually custom-built to match the unique biometric strength and arm span of each specific hunter. Their bows, known as pana, are crafted from dense anaw palm wood that must be continuously dipped in mountain rivers to preserve elasticity, paired with rot-resistant bowstrings woven from wild Ficus vine bark. To maximize hunting efficiency, they utilize a specialized arrow suite that includes aerodynamic feathered hunting arrows, multi-pronged fishing points, and heavy detachable arrowheads designed to snag on thick brush when tracking large wild boar.

fun_facts.title: River-Dipped Weaponry
fun_facts.text: Aeta hunters must continuously 'humidify' their anaw palm self-bows in flowing mountain rivers to prevent the dense wood from drying out and to maintain its explosive mechanical snapping power.

fun_facts.title: Smart Tracking Technology
fun_facts.text: The bugwan arrow features a detachable barbed metal tip connected to the main shaft by a sturdy cord, intentionally designed to separate upon impact and snag on dense jungle brush to slow down heavy game.

questions.q: What is the Aeta bow called?
What wood is the pana made from?
Why must the bow be dipped in mountain rivers?
What is the bugwan arrow designed to do?
opts: Detach and snag on jungle brush

questions.q: Aeta bowstrings are woven from:

sections.title: Olongapo Origins and Ancestors
sections.photo_caption: The ancestral roots and origins of Olongapo City
sections.content: Originally settled by the indigenous Ambala Ayta and early Malay Sambal peoples, the land that became Olongapo derived its topographical name from the Sambal words 'olang' (gentle) and 'gapo' (shoreline). The region's ethnic ancestors maintained a fierce identity, with pre-colonial Sambal warriors wielding poison-tipped arrows and kalasag shields while communicating with their creator deity, Malayari, through microtonal singing led by bayoc shamans. This history is mirrored by the legendary oral folklore of 'Ulo ng Apo,' where the tragic sight of a beloved chieftain's severed head served as a rallying cry that forged an unyielding spirit of communal unity and radical self-reliance through centuries of foreign incursions and natural disasters.

fun_facts.title: The Severed Head Symbol
fun_facts.text: According to the 'Ulo ng Apo' legend, the severed head of the beloved chieftain Apo was intentionally displayed by assassins on a bamboo pole to terrify the village, but the community instead used the tragic sight as an ultimate rallying cry to fiercely unite and protect their domain.

fun_facts.title: The Sibit-Sibit Races
fun_facts.text: The centerpiece of the city's coastal cultural celebrations revolves around fast-paced sibit-sibit boat races — named after the ultra-lightweight, traditional paddle banquitas engineered by pre-colonial ancestors for deep-water net fishing.

questions.q: What does 'olang' mean in the Sambal language?
What does 'gapo' mean in Sambal?
What is 'Ulo ng Apo'?
opts: A legend about a chieftain's severed head that became a rallying cry

questions.q: Who are the bayoc in Sambal culture?
What are sibit-sibit?
opts: Traditional paddle boats used in races

sections.title: Naval Heritage
sections.photo_caption: Subic Bay naval history and the transformation to Freeport Zone
sections.content: The strategic deep-water harbor of Subic Bay was transformed from a quiet fishing village into a major Spanish naval arsenal in 1884 by royal decree, before being claimed and expanded by the United States following the Spanish-American War. During the height of the Cold War and the Vietnam War, it exploded in size to become the single largest overseas defense facility of the U.S. military, fundamentally anchoring Olongapo's economy and turning it into a high-energy metropolis. This historic era came to an abrupt end on November 24, 1992, when the American flag was lowered for the last time following the catastrophic 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo, prompting the city to successfully pivot the base into the modern Subic Bay Freeport Zone.

fun_facts.title: The JEST Connection
fun_facts.text: During the Vietnam War era, the U.S. Navy contracted local Aeta elders to establish the Jungle Environmental Survival Training (JEST) school at Subic Bay to teach elite American forces vital survival skills.

fun_facts.title: The City of Volunteers
fun_facts.text: When the U.S. Navy unexpectedly departed after the 1991 eruption of Mount Pinatubo, over 8,000 local citizens preserved and protected the billions of dollars worth of left-behind military infrastructure without pay.

questions.q: When was Subic Bay transformed into a Spanish naval arsenal?
What training school did Aeta elders help the U.S. Navy establish?
On what date did the U.S. lower its flag at Subic Bay?
What did the former naval base become after the U.S. departure?
During which era was the Subic Bay base at its largest?
opts: The Cold War and Vietnam War

sections.title: People and Local Culture
sections.photo_caption: The vibrant people and culture of Olongapo City
sections.content: The cultural identity of Olongapo's diverse and exceptionally resilient community is anchored in a deep 'spirit of unity' that traces back to its origins as an ancient Aeta fishing village and its evolution into a bustling strategic naval base. This vibrant social tapestry seamlessly blends indigenous traditions with heavy American and Spanish historical influences, fostering an energetic urban culture celebrated today through a lively rock music scene and coastal events like the Sibit-Sibit Festival. By continuing these annual maritime traditions, modern locals actively preserve the legacy and traditional fishing culture of their ancestors.

fun_facts.title: The City of Volunteers
fun_facts.text: Following the sudden exit of the U.S. Navy and the devastating eruption of Mount Pinatubo in 1991, over 8,000 local citizens successfully protected and preserved the military facilities without pay.

fun_facts.title: The No Left Turn Rule
fun_facts.text: Olongapo pioneered a uniquely strict 'No Left Turn' traffic system across its city streets — a legacy of extreme public discipline established to transform the city's image after decades of being a rowdy, wide-open military port town.

questions.q: What festival celebrates the maritime traditions of Olongapo?
What unique traffic system did Olongapo pioneer?
Olongapo's cultural identity blends which influences?
The 'spirit of unity' in Olongapo traces back to:
opts: Its origins as an Aeta fishing village

questions.q: What aspect of modern culture thrives in Olongapo today?
